title: How to Ask For a Raise (And Actually Get It) 💰
description: Labor economists and negotiation researchers have studied which raise requests succeed and which fail. The patterns are clear, the timing matters, and most workers are doing it wrong. #SalaryNegotiation #Career #Money

segments.voice: PayScale's 2024 negotiation report found that 56 percent of workers haven't asked for a raise in the past three years. Among those who did ask, 75 percent received some form of increase. The number that gets the raise is closer to the number that asks. The conversation isn't risky in the way most people think. The risk is in not having it.

Tim Judge at the University of Notre Dame published a 2014 study tracking 30,000 workers and found that those who negotiated salary just once at the start of their career earned $600,000 more over a 40-year career than peers who didn't. One conversation, six figures of difference. The compounding comes from every subsequent raise being calculated as a percentage of the new higher base.
segments.visual: career earnings curve negotiated vs not

segments.voice: Timing matters more than the words. A 2022 Cornell study analyzed 4,400 negotiation outcomes and found requests made within two weeks after a successful project completion succeeded at 71 percent. Requests made during budget season (typically October to December at most companies) succeeded at 64 percent. Requests made during quarterly performance reviews succeeded at 49 percent. Random timing — 31 percent.

Here's the catch. Most workers ask during their annual performance review, when budgets have already been allocated. The review is the worst time to negotiate, because your manager has no flexibility left. A better strategy — schedule a separate conversation, not tied to the formal review, and frame it as a forward-looking compensation discussion.

Anchor with a specific number. Negotiation research from Adam Galinsky at Columbia shows that those who name a specific dollar amount first get 15 percent better outcomes than those who let the employer make the first offer. Counterintuitively, more precise numbers ($87,400) work better than round numbers ($90,000). Specific numbers signal you've done the research and know what you're worth.

Research the market accurately. PayScale, Glassdoor, Levels.fyi, and the BLS Occupational Employment Statistics each give a piece of the picture. The 2024 average gap between what workers think their role pays and what it actually pays is 14 percent. Most workers underestimate. Bring documented market data to the conversation. It changes the negotiation from feeling-based to data-based.

Frame contributions in dollars. A study published in the 2021 Academy of Management Journal found that raise requests that quantified specific business impact were granted at 2.3 times the rate of requests that listed accomplishments without dollar figures. I led the project becomes I led the project that generated $480,000 in new revenue. Same work. Different conversation.

What I tried in my own negotiation. I came in with a list of seven projects from the previous year. The first version was vague — increased customer satisfaction, improved processes, took on additional responsibilities. My friend who runs a recruiting firm rewrote it for me. Each item became — improved retention by 12 percent, saving an estimated $340,000 annually. The actual numbers were estimates with footnotes. The framing landed differently. Got 11 percent.

Use the silence. Most people fill awkward silences after they make their request. Negotiation research from Wharton's Maurice Schweitzer shows that the next person to speak after a number is named is the one in the weaker position. Make your case, name your number, and stop talking. The discomfort is intentional. The other person has to respond from there.

But there's a twist. The negotiation isn't only about base salary. The 2024 Mercer Total Rewards Survey found that the average worker focused only on base salary missed 23 percent of available compensation. Bonuses, equity, signing bonuses, vacation days, remote work flexibility, professional development budgets, retirement matching — all negotiable. Some are easier wins than base salary because they don't affect ongoing payroll.

Ask for something specific you'd actually do. Tuition reimbursement, conference attendance, certifications. Companies often have unused training budgets. A 2023 SHRM survey found 68 percent of companies offered tuition assistance, but only 11 percent of eligible employees used it. The ask is easy. The benefit is real. And it builds credentials that make your next negotiation stronger.

Don't lead with personal need. The single biggest mistake in raise requests is framing them around personal financial pressure. I need a raise because rent went up doesn't move the conversation forward. Your rent isn't your employer's concern. Your value to the organization is. Leading with personal need positions you as a charity case rather than a valuable asset.

Have a walk-away number. Before the conversation, decide your minimum acceptable outcome. If you don't have one, you'll accept whatever's offered. A 2019 study in the Journal of Applied Psychology found that workers who set a clear walk-away point before negotiations achieved 28 percent better outcomes than those who didn't, because they negotiated from confidence rather than fear.

Practice out loud. Wharton negotiation research found that workers who rehearsed their pitch with a friend or family member outperformed unrehearsed peers by 19 percent in actual negotiations. The first time you say a big salary number out loud should not be in front of the person who decides whether you get it. Practice makes the words feel ordinary.
segments.visual: practicing pitch in front of mirror

segments.voice: Counter-offers from outside companies are powerful but risky. The 2024 Korn Ferry leadership survey found that workers who used outside offers as leverage successfully got 8 to 22 percent raises but were also 2.4 times more likely to be quietly reorganized out within 18 months. Use this lever once, carefully, and only when you'd actually take the outside offer. It's a one-time card.
segments.visual: outside offer leverage card played once

segments.voice: Know who actually decides. In 47 percent of cases per a 2023 SHRM study, the manager you report to does not have unilateral authority to grant raises. Their boss, HR, or compensation committees often have to approve. Asking the right person at the right time matters. If your manager has to escalate the request, give them the ammunition they need to make the case upstream.

Document the path forward. If your raise request is denied, ask specifically what you'd need to do to earn the raise within six months. A 2022 study from the Wharton Center for Human Resources found that workers who got specific commitments from managers about future raise criteria earned promotions 31 percent faster than those who left meetings with vague feedback.

Email follow-up matters. After the conversation, send a written summary documenting what was discussed, what was committed to, and what you'll deliver. This serves three purposes — clarity for both parties, documentation if commitments are forgotten, and a paper trail if you need it later. A two-paragraph email after a negotiation conversation is one of the highest-leverage documents in a career.

Gender and race gaps in negotiation are real and well-documented. Hannah Riley Bowles at Harvard Kennedy School found women face roughly 30 percent backlash for negotiating assertively in ways men don't. The fix isn't telling women not to negotiate. It's adjusting the framing — using collaborative language (I want to make sure we're aligned on my role going forward) and emphasizing collective outcomes alongside individual asks.

Switch jobs strategically. The hard truth from BLS labor data — workers who change jobs every 3 to 5 years earn 50 percent more over their careers than workers who stay loyal to one company. The conversation about raises is partly a conversation about whether your current employer values you enough to keep up with the market. If they don't, the better negotiation might be elsewhere.

But staying has advantages. Tenure compounds in non-financial ways — institutional knowledge, relationships, optionality. The 2024 Gallup workplace report found that workers with 7 or more years at one employer reported 23 percent higher career satisfaction than serial job changers, after accounting for compensation. The right answer depends on your specific situation, not on a universal rule.

Bonuses are negotiable too. The 2024 Mercer survey found 41 percent of workers don't realize their bonus targets are negotiable. They're often more flexible than base salary because they don't carry forward into future budgets. Negotiating a 15 percent bonus target up to 20 percent for a key year can produce equivalent value to a permanent salary bump, with less institutional resistance.

Equity is the highest-leverage negotiation in tech and startups. A 2023 Levels.fyi analysis found that equity packages varied by 4x for the same role at the same company, depending on negotiation. Equity is the area where companies have the most flexibility because it doesn't hit current cash flow. If you're at a company where equity is meaningful, negotiate aggressively here first.

What to avoid. Threats you won't follow through on. Personal financial complaints. Vague accomplishments. Comparing yourself unfavorably to colleagues. Making it personal. Negotiating by email when in person or video is available. Each of these reduces your odds. The negotiation is a professional conversation, not a confrontation. Treat it like one.
